title: More Than a Merry-Go-Round: Smithsonian Carousel Reopens with a Civil Rights Story
summary:
After nearly three years of painstaking restoration, the Smithsonian National Carousel on the National Mall in Washington, D.C., is once again spinning with joy. But this isn't just any carousel — it's a symbol of the struggle for civil rights.

The carousel originally operated at Gwynn Oak Amusement Park outside Baltimore, Maryland. In the 1960s, that park was segregated — only white people were allowed. For years, Black and white activists protested the unfair policy. Finally, on August 28, 1963 — the very same day Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. delivered his iconic "I Have a Dream" speech on the National Mall — the park was integrated.

Sharon Langley was just 11 months old when she became the first Black child to ride the carousel that day. Now 63, she returned for the reopening and rode a horse named "Freedom Rider," a tribute to the activists who fought for equality. "This is a monument for children to come and enjoy, ride and experience the pursuit of happiness," she said.

Janice Chance, who first rode the carousel in 1966 at age 13, also attended. Her son, a Marine, died in Afghanistan in 2008. She said the carousel's return means a lot to those who fought for "the freedoms of this country."

The restored carousel features 54 horses, a sea monster, a pig, and two chariots, including a new ADA-compliant chariot (accessible for people with disabilities). Kids wasted no time jumping on. Seven-year-old Lucas Platt from Virginia gave it high marks: "It's actually one of the fastest carousels I've really been on. Usually they're much slower than this. It's great."

After Hurricane Agnes damaged Gwynn Oak in 1973, the carousel went into storage. Then Smithsonian Secretary S. Dillon Ripley decided it was time for a grand replacement on the National Mall. Today, it stands in front of the Smithsonian's Arts and Industries Building, a joyful reminder that fun and fairness can go hand in hand.
why_it_matters: For 12-14 year olds, this story shows that historical objects are not just old things — they carry powerful stories about justice and change. Understanding the civil rights era helps you see why fighting for equality remains relevant today, whether in schools, public spaces, or online.

keywords.explanation: The institution that restored and operates the carousel on the National Mall.
A historic merry-go-round that served as a civil rights symbol; features 54 horses, a sea monster, a pig, and two chariots.
The location in Washington, D.C., where the carousel stands, in front of the Arts and Industries Building.
The former amusement park in Maryland where the carousel originally operated; it was segregated until 1963.
The first Black child to ride the carousel when Gwynn Oak was integrated; now 63, she returned for the reopening.
The policy of separating people by race; Gwynn Oak was segregated, allowing only white visitors until protests changed it.

It was sold to a private collector.

background_read: The civil rights movement of the 1950s and 1960s fought to end racial segregation in the United States. Protests, sit-ins, and marches, including the 1963 March on Washington where Dr. King gave his 'I Have a Dream' speech, pressured the government to pass the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the Voting Rights Act of 1965.

Gwynn Oak Amusement Park was one of many public facilities that barred Black visitors. On August 28, 1963, after years of protests by activists including the Baltimore-based group 'CORE' (Congress of Racial Equality), the park finally opened to all. That same day, over 250,000 people gathered at the Lincoln Memorial for the March on Washington.

The carousel itself dates back to the early 20th century and was built by the Philadelphia Toboggan Company. After Hurricane Agnes damaged Gwynn Oak in 1973, the carousel was stored before Smithsonian Secretary S. Dillon Ripley acquired it. It was installed on the National Mall in 1981 and has since become a beloved landmark. The recent restoration took nearly three years and included adding an ADA-compliant chariot to make the ride accessible for people with disabilities.

└─ Sharon Langley becomes first Black child to ride.

├─ Quote: 'This is a monument for children to come and enjoy, ride and experience the pursuit of happiness.'
└─ Janice Chance (rode in 1966) also attends; her son died in Afghanistan.
├─ 54 horses, sea monster, pig, two chariots.
├─ Hurricane Agnes damaged Gwynn Oak in 1973; carousel went into storage.
└─ Smithsonian Secretary S. Dillon Ripley acquired it for the National Mall.
KIDS’ REACTION: Lucas Platt: 'It's actually one of the fastest carousels I've really been on... It's great.'
OPEN QUESTION: How can public monuments continue to teach history?

perspectives.description: The carousel’s reopening is a joyful event that honors the progress made in civil rights. Sharon Langley’s return, the ADA-compliant chariot, and children riding again show how history can bring communities together.

Some might argue that while a carousel is a nice symbol, it doesn’t address ongoing racial inequality in the U.S. The story could risk oversimplifying the struggle into a feel-good moment, ignoring that many schools and neighborhoods are still segregated today.

This carousel provides a tangible link to a key moment in the civil rights movement. Historians note that integrating public spaces like amusement parks was a major grassroots victory. The restoration effort preserves that context for future generations.

The carousel can be a starting point for deeper conversations about equality. Schools and families can use its story to discuss how far we’ve come and what still needs to change, from public accommodations to economic opportunity.
